frames.caption: Goal: serialize ["lc", "le#et", ""] into one string and recover it exactly. Note "le#et" CONTAINS the # marker, so a plain join on # would be ambiguous — we need length-prefix framing.
frames.note: A single-character delimiter (comma, #, newline) is unsafe the moment that character can appear inside the data, as it does in "le#et". Length-prefix framing sidesteps the problem entirely: prefix every string with its length so the decoder is told exactly how far to read, never having to guess where a value ends.

frames.caption: Encode strs[0] = "lc" → frame "2#lc": write length 2, then '#', then the 2 chars. encoded = "2#lc".
frames.note: Each string is framed as <len>#<payload>: write its character count, a non-digit marker (#), then the raw characters. The length prefix is what makes this delimiter-safe — unlike joining on a single character, the payload may itself contain #, digits, or newlines and there is still no ambiguity about where one string ends and the next begins.

frames.caption: Encode strs[1] = "le#et" → frame "5#le#et": write length 5, then '#', then the 5 chars. encoded = "2#lc5#le#et".
frames.note: Each string is framed as <len>#<payload>: write its character count, a non-digit marker (#), then the raw characters. The length prefix is what makes this delimiter-safe — unlike joining on a single character, the payload may itself contain #, digits, or newlines and there is still no ambiguity about where one string ends and the next begins.

frames.caption: Encode strs[2] = "" → frame "0#": write length 0, then '#', no payload. encoded = "2#lc5#le#et0#".
frames.note: The empty string is not a special case here: its length is 0, so it frames as "0#" with no payload. That uniformity is the elegance of length-prefix framing — "0#" round-trips back to "" with the same code path, and an empty input list simply produces the empty encoded string.

frames.caption: Encoding complete: "2#lc5#le#et0#". This single string holds all three originals unambiguously — now decode it back.
frames.note: The produced string is self-describing: every value is preceded by its own length, so no external schema or escaping is needed. Encode is O(N) over the total characters, and the result is O(N) space — the minimum needed to hold the data plus its small per-string length prefixes.

frames.caption: Switch to decode. Walk the encoded string left to right: at each frame boundary i, read the length digits, slice that many chars, then jump past the frame.
frames.note: Decode reverses encode with the same O(N) cost. The cursor i always sits on a frame boundary — the first character of a length prefix — so each iteration independently parses one <len>#<payload> unit without needing any state from the previous one.

frames.caption: i=0: scan digits up to '#' at index 1 → "2", so len = 2.
frames.note: Decoding a frame is the mirror of encoding it. Start at the frame boundary i and scan forward to the first #; everything between is the length in decimal. We never assume a single digit — "10#..." or "274#..." are read the same way — so the marker, not a fixed width, terminates the count.

frames.caption: Slice 2 chars after the '#' (indices 2..3): word = "lc". Decoded so far: ["lc"].
frames.note: With the length parsed, slice exactly that many characters starting right after the #. This is the step that defeats a naive delimiter: because we read a fixed count rather than scanning for the next #, the embedded # inside "le#et" is consumed as ordinary data and never mistaken for a separator.

frames.caption: Jump past the frame: i = 1 + 1 + 2 = 4. Continue from the next frame boundary.
frames.note: Advance i past the whole frame — over the digits, the #, and the len payload characters (i = j + 1 + len) — so the next iteration starts cleanly on the following frame boundary. Repeating this until i reaches the end recovers the original list exactly, in O(N) over the total characters.

frames.caption: i=4: scan digits up to '#' at index 5 → "5", so len = 5.
frames.note: Decoding a frame is the mirror of encoding it. Start at the frame boundary i and scan forward to the first #; everything between is the length in decimal. We never assume a single digit — "10#..." or "274#..." are read the same way — so the marker, not a fixed width, terminates the count.

frames.caption: Slice 5 chars after the '#' (indices 6..10): word = "le#et" — note the embedded # is data, not a boundary. Decoded so far: ["lc", "le#et"].
frames.note: With the length parsed, slice exactly that many characters starting right after the #. This is the step that defeats a naive delimiter: because we read a fixed count rather than scanning for the next #, the embedded # inside "le#et" is consumed as ordinary data and never mistaken for a separator.

frames.caption: Jump past the frame: i = 5 + 1 + 5 = 11. Continue from the next frame boundary.
frames.note: Advance i past the whole frame — over the digits, the #, and the len payload characters (i = j + 1 + len) — so the next iteration starts cleanly on the following frame boundary. Repeating this until i reaches the end recovers the original list exactly, in O(N) over the total characters.

frames.caption: i=11: scan digits up to '#' at index 12 → "0", so len = 0.
frames.note: Decoding a frame is the mirror of encoding it. Start at the frame boundary i and scan forward to the first #; everything between is the length in decimal. We never assume a single digit — "10#..." or "274#..." are read the same way — so the marker, not a fixed width, terminates the count.

frames.caption: len = 0 → slice 0 chars after the '#': word = "". Decoded so far: ["lc", "le#et", ""].
frames.note: The empty string is not a special case here: its length is 0, so it frames as "0#" with no payload. That uniformity is the elegance of length-prefix framing — "0#" round-trips back to "" with the same code path, and an empty input list simply produces the empty encoded string.

frames.caption: Jump past the frame: i = 12 + 1 + 0 = 13 = end of string. Decoding done.
frames.note: Advance i past the whole frame — over the digits, the #, and the len payload characters (i = j + 1 + len) — so the next iteration starts cleanly on the following frame boundary. Repeating this until i reaches the end recovers the original list exactly, in O(N) over the total characters.

frames.caption: Decoded back to ["lc", "le#et", ""] — identical to the input, embedded '#' and empty string included. Length-prefix framing is lossless.
frames.note: The recovered list matches the original byte for byte, including the # inside "le#et" and the empty string. That round-trip guarantee is the contract length-prefix framing provides, and it holds for ANY payload because the length — not a fragile delimiter — defines each boundary.
